section.title: The Altar and the Courtyard Built
section.sectionRationale: Bezalel completes the bronze altar and the courtyard that surrounds the tabernacle, including a striking note that the bronze basin was made from mirrors donated by serving women, and the chapter closes with a full, transparent inventory of all the gold, silver, and bronze used in the entire project.
section.thesis: Bezalel builds the bronze altar, the basin from women's mirrors, and the linen courtyard exactly as commanded, and Moses records a precise, public accounting of every talent and shekel of gold, silver, and bronze used in the tabernacle's construction.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the bronze altar of burnt offering; the bronze basin made from the mirrors of serving women; the courtyard enclosing the tabernacle; and the full, itemized inventory of gold, silver, and bronze.

units.label: The bronze altar
units.note: boards, so that it was hollow

media.alt: A bronze basin reflecting light near a tent entrance
media.caption: The bronze basin is cast from the mirrors of women who served at the tent entrance.

verses.text: Bezalel constructed the altar of burnt offering from acacia wood. It was square, five cubits long, five cubits wide, and three cubits high.
He made a horn at each of its four corners, so that the horns and altar were of one piece, and he overlaid the altar with bronze.
He made all the altar's utensils of bronze, its pots, shovels, sprinkling bowls, meat forks, and firepans.
He made a grate of bronze mesh for the altar under its ledge, halfway up from the bottom.
At the four corners of the bronze grate he cast four rings as holders for the poles.
And he made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with bronze.
Then he inserted the poles into the rings on the sides of the altar for carrying it. He made the altar with boards so that it was hollow.

units.label: The basin from the mirrors
units.note: the women who served at the entrance

verses.text: Next he made the bronze basin and its stand from the {{mirrors|mirrors}} of the women who served at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
Then he constructed the courtyard. The south side of the courtyard was a hundred cubits long and had curtains of finely spun linen,
with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and with silver hooks and bands on the posts.
The north side was also a hundred cubits long, with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases. The hooks and bands of the posts were silver.
The west side was fifty cubits long and had curtains, with ten posts and ten bases. The hooks and bands of the posts were silver.
And the east side, toward the sunrise, was also fifty cubits long.
The curtains on one side of the entrance were fifteen cubits long, with three posts and three bases.
And the curtains on the other side were also fifteen cubits long, with three posts and three bases as well.
All the curtains around the courtyard were made of finely spun linen.
The bases for the posts were bronze, the hooks and bands were silver, and the plating for the tops of the posts was silver. So all the posts of the courtyard were banded with silver.

The curtain for the entrance to the courtyard was embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen. It was twenty cubits long and, like the curtains of the courtyard, five cubits high,
with four posts and four bronze bases. Their hooks were silver, as well as the bands and the plating of their tops.
All the tent pegs for the tabernacle and for the surrounding courtyard were bronze.

units.label: The inventory
units.note: everything just as the LORD had commanded Moses

verses.text: This is the {{inventory|inventory}} for the tabernacle, the tabernacle of the Testimony, as recorded at Moses' command by the Levites under the direction of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest.
Bezalel son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made everything that the LORD had commanded Moses.
With him was Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, an engraver, designer, and embroiderer in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine linen.
All the gold from the wave offering used for the work on the sanctuary totaled 29 talents and 730 shekels, according to the sanctuary shekel.
The silver from those numbered among the congregation totaled 100 talents and 1,775 shekels, according to the sanctuary shekel,
a beka per person, that is, half a shekel, according to the sanctuary shekel, from everyone twenty years of age or older who had crossed over to be numbered, a total of 603,550 men.
The hundred talents of silver were used to cast the bases of the sanctuary and the bases of the veil, 100 bases from the 100 talents, one talent per base.
With the 1,775 shekels of silver he made the hooks for the posts, overlaid their tops, and supplied bands for them.
The bronze from the wave offering totaled 70 talents and 2,400 shekels.
He used it to make the bases for the entrance to the Tent of Meeting, the bronze altar and its bronze grating, all the utensils for the altar,
the bases for the surrounding courtyard and its gate, and all the tent pegs for the tabernacle and its surrounding courtyard.

mirrors.gloss: Mirrors, polished bronze surfaces used for one's reflection. Women who served at the tent entrance gave up these valuable personal items to be melted down into the basin priests used for washing, turning an object of self-regard into an instrument of purification.

inventory.gloss: An accounting, inventory, or reckoning. This detailed public record of every talent and shekel used in the tabernacle's construction demonstrates transparent, accountable stewardship of what the people had generously given to God.

basic.context: Bezalel finishes the bronze altar and the basin, remarkably made from the bronze mirrors of women who served at the tent entrance, then completes the linen courtyard surrounding the whole structure. The chapter closes with a full, public accounting of exactly how much gold, silver, and bronze was used, down to the shekel.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a historical record of construction and careful financial accounting, not a text requiring symbolic decoding at every point. Its significance lies in two clear, plain truths: sacrificial giving, even of personal items like mirrors, and transparent stewardship of what God's people had entrusted for His work.
basic.originalLanguages: The basin was cast from mar'ah, mirrors, belonging to women serving at the tabernacle entrance, a striking detail of personal sacrifice turned toward purification. The chapter's detailed pequdim, inventory or accounting, shows every talent and shekel of gold, silver, and bronze publicly recorded and traced to its use.
basic.typology: The mirrors given up for the basin of washing anticipate the way believers now surrender self-regard for the sake of purification and service, looking not at their own reflection but toward Christ. The public, transparent inventory anticipates the New Testament's emphasis on trustworthy, accountable stewardship of what God's people give for His work.
basic.christ: Paul writes that believers now behold the Lord's glory as in a mirror and are transformed into the same image, 2 Corinthians 3:18, using imagery that echoes this very basin, turning attention away from self and toward Christ's glory. The careful, honest accounting of the offering foreshadows the integrity Paul insists on in handling gifts given for the Lord's work, 2 Corinthians 8:20-21.
basic.god: God cares about ordinary, personal sacrifices, women's mirrors becoming an instrument of purification, and He cares about careful, honest accounting of what has been given to Him. Nothing about serving God, however small or however financial, is exempt from both wholehearted devotion and scrupulous integrity.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Corinthians 3:18 speaks of believers beholding the Lord's glory as in a mirror and being transformed. 2 Corinthians 8:20-21 insists on transparent, honest handling of financial gifts given for ministry. 1 Corinthians 4:2 says stewards are required to be found faithful.
basic.oneStory: Women give up their mirrors, instruments of self-regard, to make a basin for purification, and Moses ensures every gift given for the tabernacle is publicly, honestly accounted for. This pattern of sacrificial devotion and transparent stewardship continues in the church, where believers turn from self toward Christ and handle what is given for His work with integrity.

context.literary: This chapter completes the construction narrative begun in chapter 36, moving from the altar and basin to the surrounding courtyard, then closing the entire tabernacle project with a formal, itemized financial summary rarely paralleled elsewhere in the Pentateuch's narrative sections.
context.historical: The half shekel figure and total of 603,550 men correspond directly to the census ransom commanded in Exodus 30:11-16 and the population figures recorded in Numbers 1:46, confirming this accounting reflects a real, verifiable historical collection.
context.canonical: This detailed financial transparency becomes a model echoed in later biblical accounts of temple construction and repair, such as under Joash and Josiah, where careful accounting of funds given for God's house is likewise emphasized as essential to faithful stewardship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the completion of the tabernacle's altar, basin, and courtyard, and to provide a transparent, verifiable accounting of the materials used, demonstrating both the people's sacrificial generosity and the leadership's careful integrity in handling what was given.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical narrative describing a specific construction project and its financial accounting; it does not prescribe an exact bookkeeping format for churches today. Its abiding truth is the value of both sacrificial giving and transparent, accountable stewardship of what is given for God's work.

debates.question: Why does the text specifically note that the basin was made from women's mirrors, rather than simply saying it was made of bronze?

views.label: Highlighting sacrificial devotion
views.case: Many interpreters see this detail as intentionally highlighting the depth of these women's devotion, giving up items tied to personal vanity or self-image for the sake of an instrument of purification used in approaching God.

views.label: A specific historical note without deeper symbolic intent
views.case: Others read this more simply as an accurate historical record of an available and valuable source of bronze, without necessarily intending the deeper symbolic contrast between vanity and purification, since the text itself does not explicitly draw that moral lesson.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same underlying fact: real, personal items of value were willingly given by women for the tabernacle's construction, whatever the precise weight of symbolic intent behind the detail's inclusion.

study.originalLanguages: The basin's bronze came from mar'ah, mirrors, belonging to women who served at the tabernacle entrance, a concrete, personal form of sacrificial giving. The chapter's closing pequdim, inventory, meticulously records every measure of gold, silver, and bronze, demonstrating that generous devotion and careful accountability were expected to go hand in hand.

typology.type: Mirrors surrendered for an instrument of purification
typology.fulfillment: Believers who turn from self-regard to behold Christ's glory and be transformed into His likeness, 2 Corinthians 3:18.

typology.type: The transparent, itemized accounting of the offering
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament's insistence on honest, accountable handling of gifts given for gospel ministry, 2 Corinthians 8:20-21.

study.christ: Paul's image of believers beholding the Lord's glory as in a mirror and being transformed into the same image, 2 Corinthians 3:18, resonates with this basin's origin: an object once used for self-reflection repurposed for purification before God, anticipating how believers now look away from themselves and toward Christ. The scrupulous accounting Moses provides here models the integrity Paul later insists upon in handling gifts given for Christ's church, 2 Corinthians 8:20-21.
study.god: God receives even the smallest, most personal sacrifices, women's mirrors, as fitting material for His service, showing that no gift offered in devotion is too ordinary for His purposes. At the same time, God's work is handled with complete transparency, every talent and shekel accounted for, showing that sincere devotion to God and careful integrity in stewardship belong together, not in tension.

crossReferences.note: Believers behold the Lord's glory as in a mirror and are transformed into His likeness.
Paul insists on transparent, honest handling of gifts given for ministry.
It is required of stewards that they be found faithful.

study.oneStory: Women give up their mirrors to become an instrument of purification, and Moses ensures every gift given for God's dwelling is honestly, publicly accounted for. This pattern of sacrificial, personal devotion joined to careful stewardship continues in the church, where believers look away from themselves toward Christ's glory and handle what is entrusted to them with integrity.

quiz.q: What material made up the bronze basin and its stand, according to verse 8?

options.text: The mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
options.note: Verse 8 specifies this exact, striking source of the bronze.

options.text: Bronze shields captured from the Amalekites.
options.note: No such captured shields are mentioned; the text specifically names women's mirrors as the source.

options.text: Ordinary cooking pots donated by the community.
options.note: The specific source named is mirrors, not cooking pots.

options.text: No source is given for the basin's material.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly names the women's mirrors as the source.

quiz.q: What material formed the bases of the courtyard posts, and what material formed their hooks and bands?

options.text: Bronze bases with silver hooks and bands.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 and 17 specify this exact combination throughout the courtyard.

options.text: Gold bases with bronze hooks and bands.
options.note: Gold was reserved for the more sacred interior furnishings; the courtyard posts specifically had bronze bases and silver hooks.

options.text: Wooden bases with no metal fittings.
options.note: The bases were specifically bronze, with silver hooks and bands, not plain wood.

options.text: No materials are specified for the posts.
options.note: Verses 10 through 17 explicitly detail bronze bases with silver hooks and bands.

quiz.q: Who was given oversight of recording the tabernacle's inventory, according to verse 21?

options.text: The Levites, under the direction of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest.
options.note: Verse 21 names this specific oversight structure.

options.text: Joshua son of Nun.
options.note: Joshua served as Moses' personal attendant, not as the one overseeing this specific inventory.

options.text: The seventy elders alone.
options.note: The elders had a different role in Exodus 24; the inventory oversight is specifically credited to the Levites under Ithamar.

options.text: No one is credited with this record.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly names the Levites and Ithamar's oversight.

quiz.q: How much gold was used for the tabernacle's construction, according to verse 24?

options.text: 29 talents and 730 shekels, according to the sanctuary shekel.
options.note: Verse 24 states this exact figure.

options.text: 100 talents and 1,775 shekels.
options.note: This figure describes the silver total in verse 25, not the gold total.

options.text: 70 talents and 2,400 shekels.
options.note: This figure describes the bronze total in verse 29, not the gold total.

options.text: No amount of gold is specified.
options.note: Verse 24 explicitly states the exact gold total.

quiz.q: How was the silver total of 100 talents and 1,775 shekels collected, according to verses 25 to 26?

options.text: As a half shekel census payment from every man twenty years or older, 603,550 men in total.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 explicitly connect this silver total to the census ransom.

options.text: As a voluntary gift from only the wealthiest Israelites.
options.note: The silver came specifically from the equal, mandatory census ransom, not a voluntary gift limited to the wealthy.

options.text: As tribute paid by defeated Canaanite nations.
options.note: No tribute from foreign nations is described; the silver came from Israel's own census payment.

options.text: No explanation is given for the silver's collection.
options.note: Verses 25 to 26 explicitly explain the silver came from the census ransom.

questions: Women gave up their personal mirrors, valuable and intimate items, to become part of a basin used for purification before God. What personal possession or attachment might God be asking you to surrender for the sake of His work?
The tabernacle's finances were meticulously, publicly accounted for down to the shekel. How transparent and accountable is your own handling of money or resources given for God's purposes?

The bronze basin, once used for looking at oneself, became an instrument for washing before serving God. Where in your life could something once focused on self be repurposed toward serving and honoring God instead?

The silver came from an equal ransom paid by every man in Israel, rich or poor alike. How does remembering that your own place among God's people rests on an equal, not merit-based, provision shape your sense of belonging?

This chapter closes an enormous building project with careful bookkeeping rather than a dramatic celebration. Where in your own life do you need to value the quiet, careful, administrative faithfulness that keeps God's work honest and trustworthy?

translationNotes.note: The mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting is a brief but notable detail; these were likely polished bronze surfaces used as mirrors, and the identity and specific service of these women is not further elaborated here, though similar language appears in 1 Samuel 2:22.

The tabernacle of the Testimony emphasizes the ark and its covenant tablets as the defining feature of the whole structure, even as this verse turns to a detailed financial inventory rather than further furnishing description.
The total of 603,550 men corresponds exactly to the census figure recorded in Numbers 1:46, confirming this financial accounting reflects the same historical census commanded in Exodus 30:11-16.
